story:
Chained to a floor ring and shoved into a dim interview room, I bare my fangs at a trembling guard until a small, expensively dressed man cuts in. He calls himself “Mr. R, Attorney at Law,” slides a card stamped with a golden spinning wheel and orders the guard out. He doesn’t smell like fear. That means he’s either a fool—or a villain. Like me.

Mr. R says I haven’t hired him yet, but I will once I hear his pitch: he can keep me from spending my life in a cage. I scoff. The case against me is solid, and I don’t pretend I’m innocent. He presses for my side anyway, reminding me that attorney-client talk stays private. So I say it straight: yeah, I did it. I stalked Peter and his friends. I hunted a duck. I blew down two of the pigs’ houses (not my fault they weren’t to code) and they almost roasted me for it. I ate a girl in a red hood and her grandmother. And—people forget—the cat. The woodsman cut me open, stuffed me with stones, and I woke on a riverbank, endured surgeries, and spent six months in a humiliating cone. If not for the woodsman, I’d still be out there doing what I do.

Mr. R just polishes his glasses. No flinch. He says proving my innocence isn’t his aim. He’ll reframe me as a victim, not a villain. We’ll play up prejudice—the way people see “wolf” and run. He asks about a mate, pups, a pack that needs me. None of that. He waves it off. Facts can be spun. Cameras love a sob story. He’ll get me a short sentence and, afterward, a cozy den, private woods, food, peace. He specializes in deals. And he gets fifty percent of the money from interviews, TV, and a book.

It makes my hackles rise. He wants me to lie, to pretend I’m helpless so I can dodge the weight of what I’ve done, while he cashes in. He needles me: haven’t I lied before? Impersonating a little old lady isn’t exactly honest. A predator’s cunning is one thing, I say, but this feels worse. He clicks open a briefcase, flourishes a gold pen, and pushes papers my way. All I have to do is sign, and he’ll spin me into a tragic hero.

Here’s the thing: I am a villain. I chose it. I owned it—right up until I got caught. Now a man who reeks of slick promises wants to twist the story, not to help me change, but to sell me as a product. He’s a worse kind of villain: the kind who turns lies into currency.

I tell him no. He should leave before I start naming names—or settle this the old way. He stalls, so I lunge. Chain tight, paws on the table, bark sharp enough to rattle the bulb. He falls, scrambles, finally smells like fear, and bolts for the door.

Hand on the knob, he throws one last line: they’ll always see you as a villain. I tell him maybe I earned that. If I want it to change, I’ll have to change—by doing the right thing, not by pretending I already have. Even a villain can choose a better ending. Maybe that’s the moral.
